claim: 95% of enterprise AI projects fail according to a viral MIT study, but the actual finding is that 2/3 were internal projects or used consultants while 1/3 used external vendors, with the external vendor category having significantly higher success rates.
actionable: When evaluating AI project success rates, recognize that failure metrics depend heavily on implementation approach—buying from external vendors outperforms internal builds and consulting engagements.
source_title: Good News For Startups: Enterprise Is Bad At AI

claim: Enterprise software failures stem from engineers not believing in AI and not using code generation tools, combined with distrust of new solutions—creating opportunity for startups that build working AI products.
actionable: As a startup founder, focus on winning deals with enterprises where internal teams have skill gaps or skepticism—you only need a superior product when they have 'no other options.'
source_title: Good News For Startups: Enterprise Is Bad At AI

claim: Successful enterprise software requires product excellence, polymaths with both engineering and domain understanding, and embedding into business processes—not just plug-and-play solutions.
actionable: Build enterprise AI products by hiring founders/leads who understand both deep technical implementation and domain expertise, and expect integration timelines of months to years, not weeks.
source_title: Good News For Startups: Enterprise Is Bad At AI

claim: Enterprise switching costs become prohibitive once invested in training an AI system, creating a durable moat for AI SaaS companies that successfully implement solutions.
actionable: When pitching to enterprises, emphasize the strategic advantage of being first to train their AI system, as this creates high switching costs that protect your market position long-term.
source_title: Good News For Startups: Enterprise Is Bad At AI

claim: YC partners report engineering teams at large enterprises filled with skeptics who distrust AI, creating opportunity for YC's top 1% of founders to become part of the top 5% of successful implementations.
actionable: If you're building AI products at scale, view enterprise skepticism not as a barrier but as a sign the market is underserved—focus on solving for the internal champions who want to succeed.
source_title: Good News For Startups: Enterprise Is Bad At AI

claim: Finding a 'startup-dreamer' employee inside an enterprise who wants to vicariously experience startup culture can unlock deals—they live through the founder's journey and actively support implementation.
actionable: When entering enterprise accounts, identify and cultivate relationships with employees who have startup aspirations but are risk-averse—they become your most effective internal advocates.
source_title: Good News For Startups: Enterprise Is Bad At AI

claim: Reducto closed a $154M+ fan company deal 154 days post-YC launch by becoming friends with the internal champion, overcoming internal solutions that had failed with open-source and AWS Tesseract OCR.
actionable: Build deep personal relationships with champions, don't scale, and understand that product excellence combined with genuine human connection wins against entrenched internal solutions.
source_title: Good News For Startups: Enterprise Is Bad At AI
